## Why transformers changed NLP

Earlier NLP models had a basic representation problem: a word such as `bank`
received the same vector regardless of context. That is often good enough for
classical bag-of-words methods, but it breaks down once meaning depends on distant
words in the sentence.

Transformers solved this by building `contextual` token representations. Instead of
assigning one fixed embedding to each word type, they update each token
representation based on the surrounding sequence.

## Self-attention is the core mechanism

The key idea is simple: when computing the representation of one token, the model
should be able to look at other tokens in the sequence and decide which ones matter
most.

That mechanism is `self-attention`. For each position, the model:

1. compares the current token with other tokens,
2. assigns attention weights,
3. and combines information from the relevant tokens into a new contextualized
   representation.

This is what lets the model decide that `bank` is related to `water` in one
sentence and to `credit` in another.

## Query, key, and value

The legacy Lecture 8 slides describe self-attention through three learned
projections:

- `query`: what the current token is looking for,
- `key`: what each token offers as an address or match signal,
- `value`: the content to be retrieved if the match is strong.

If the input embedding at position `i` is `x_i`, then the model learns:

$$
q_i = x_i W^Q,\qquad k_i = x_i W^K,\qquad v_i = x_i W^V
$$

The attention score between token `i` and token `j` is based on the scaled dot
product between the query and key:

$$
\operatorname{score}(i,j) = \frac{q_i \cdot k_j}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

After a softmax step, these scores become weights that sum to one, and the new
representation is a weighted combination of the value vectors.

## Why self-attention is powerful

The attraction of self-attention is not only accuracy. It changes what is easy for
the model to learn.

- Long-range dependencies are easier to capture because any token can attend
  directly to any other token in the sequence.
- Training is more parallelizable than recurrent sequence models.
- The attention weights offer at least some interpretive signal about what parts of
  the sequence influenced a representation.

This is why transformers became the dominant architecture behind modern LLMs.

## Causal and bidirectional attention are different choices

Not every transformer is allowed to see the same context.

- In `causal` attention, a token can only attend to previous tokens. This is the
  setup used for next-token prediction in GPT-style models.
- In `bidirectional` attention, a token can attend to tokens on both sides. This is
  common in encoder-style models such as BERT.

The causal case requires masking: the model must not peek at future tokens during
training, or the prediction task becomes trivial.

## Multi-head attention learns several relevance patterns at once

One attention pattern is often too restrictive. Different relationships may matter
simultaneously: syntax, entity reference, negation, topic cues, and many others.

`Multi-head attention` handles this by running several attention mechanisms in
parallel on different learned projections of the same inputs. The outputs are then
combined.

The practical effect is greater representational capacity. Different heads can focus
on different kinds of structure in the same sentence.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students to imagine two simultaneous questions for the same token: "Which word
does this refer to syntactically?" and "Which earlier words define the topic?" That
is the simplest intuition for why one attention pattern is not enough.
:::

## A transformer block is more than attention

A standard transformer block includes three important pieces around attention:

- a residual connection,
- layer normalization,
- and a feed-forward network.

The block logic is roughly:

1. compute multi-head attention,
2. add the original signal back through a residual path,
3. normalize,
4. apply a feed-forward network,
5. add another residual path,
6. normalize again.

The residual paths and normalization mainly help optimization and training stability.
The feed-forward stage adds non-linearity and richer feature transformation beyond
the weighted averaging of attention alone.

## From static to contextual embeddings

Word2Vec gave one vector per word. Transformers give one vector per token position
in context. That is the conceptual leap that connects Lecture 6 to Lecture 8.

It is also why transformers work so well for ambiguous language: the model does not
need to store one universal meaning for `bank`. It can build the meaning required by
the current sentence.

## What to report

When students explain attention and transformers in this course, they should
report:

- what self-attention is doing conceptually,
- how query, key, and value differ,
- why masking is needed for causal language modelling,
- why multi-head attention is useful,
- and one clear reason transformers improved on static embedding approaches.
